messages.content: # Section 302 IPC: Definition, Punishment, and Judicial Interpretation **Applicable law:** Indian Penal Code, 1860 — Section 302 ## Direct Answer **[ESTABLISHED]** Section 302 IPC punishes the offence of murder with either death penalty or imprisonment for life, along with a fine [India Code:1][Indian Kanoon:3]. The punishment is discretionary—no mandatory sentence is prescribed. The offence is defined as the commission of murder, which is delineated by Section 300 IPC (not reproduced in sources but statutorily linked to Section 302) [India Code:2](snippet only — unverified). ## Key Points - **Definition:** Murder under Section 302 IPC is statutorily tied to the definition of murder in Section 300 IPC (actus reus + mens rea: intention or knowledge of causing death or such bodily injury as is likely to cause death) [India Code:2](snippet only — unverified). - **Punishment:** Punishment is either death or imprisonment for life, plus a fine. The statute does not mandate which punishment applies; judicial discretion governs [India Code:1][Indian Kanoon:3]. - **Ingredients:** The *essential* ingredients are (i) causing death of a person, and (ii) the act being done with the intention of causing death or knowledge that it is likely to cause death. Without *mens rea*, the offence may fall under culpable homicide (Section 299/304 IPC) [Indian Kanoon:8](snippet only — unverified). - **Judicial Interpretation:** Courts interpret murder under Section 302 IPC by examining the *mens rea* and the actus reus, and apply exceptions under Section 300 (e.g., grave and sudden provocation, exceeding right of private defence) to reduce the charge to culpable homicide not amounting to murder (Section 304) [Indian Kanoon:9](snippet only — unverified); [Indian Kanoon:10](snippet only — unverified). ## Analysis The statutory scheme under the IPC distinguishes between murder (Section 302) and culpable homicide (Sections 299–304) based on the *mens rea* and aggravating circumstances. While Section 302 punishes murder with severe penalties, courts frequently examine whether the accused’s actions fall within exceptions to murder (e.g., provocation or private defence), which may reduce the offence to culpable homicide under Section 304 IPC [Indian Kanoon:9](snippet only — unverified). This interpretive approach ensures proportionality in sentencing and avoids over-criminalisation in borderline cases. The punishment structure—death or life imprisonment—reflects the legislature’s recognition that murder is among the gravest offences, but leaves room for judicial nuance in individual cases. The discretion is not unfettered; courts must apply statutory exceptions and principles of proportionality [India Code:1][Indian Kanoon:3]. ## Practical Note If you are drafting a charge sheet or framing an offence, ensure the prosecution establishes *mens rea* (intention or knowledge) beyond reasonable doubt. If mitigating factors exist (e.g., sudden provocation, lack of premeditation), consider charging under Section 304 IPC instead of Section 302 IPC [Indian Kanoon:8](snippet only — unverified); [Indian Kanoon:9](snippet only — unverified). For sentencing, be prepared to justify the choice of punishment (death vs. life imprisonment) based on aggravating and mitigating factors as per judicial precedents. --- *This is AI-assisted legal research; consult a lawyer for advice.* ## Suggested Follow-up Queries 1. What are the essential ingredients to differentiate murder (Section 302 IPC) from culpable homicide not amounting to murder (Section 304 IPC)? 2. What judicial principles guide the imposition of the death penalty in murder cases under Section 302 IPC? 3. How does Section 300 IPC define murder for the purpose of Section 302 IPC? 4. What are the procedural safeguards in murder trials under Section 302 IPC as per the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973? > **Statute Verification Warning**: The following section number(s) appear in India Code citations but were not confirmed in the fetched statute text — §300. Verify the exact text at indiacode.nic.in before relying on these references. ## Table of Authorities **Statutes** [India Code:1] [Access Denied](https://www.indiacode.nic.in/show-data?actid=AC_CEN_5_23_00037_186045_1523266765688&orderno=338) ✅ fetched [India Code:2] [1501764525-THE-INDIAN-PENAL-CODE-1860](https://www.indiacode.nic.in/bitstream/123456789/4219/1/THE-INDIAN-PENAL-CODE-1860.pdf) ⚠️ snippet only **Case Law** [Indian Kanoon:3] [Indian Kanoon - Search engine for Indian Law](https://indiankanoon.org/search/?formInput=section+302+ipc) ✅ fetched [Indian Kanoon:4] [Indian Kanoon - Search engine for Indian Law](https://indiankanoon.org/doc/1560742/) ✅ fetched [Indian Kanoon:5] [Indian Kanoon - Search engine for Indian Law](https://indiankanoon.org/browse/) ✅ fetched [Indian Kanoon:6] [Indian Kanoon - Search engine for Indian Law](https://indiankanoon.org/index.html) ✅ fetched [Indian Kanoon:7] [Indian Kanoon - Search engine for Indian Law](https://indiankanoon.org/) ⚠️ snippet only [Indian Kanoon:8] [Manoj Singh vs State Of M.P. on 10 November, 2021 - Indian Kanoon](https://indiankanoon.org/docfragment/128584680/?formInput=murder+ingredients) ⚠️ snippet only [Indian Kanoon:9] [Bodha @ Ramesh Turi & Ors vs The State Of West Bengal & Anr ...](https://indiankanoon.org/doc/167990544/) ⚠️ snippet only [Indian Kanoon:10] [Section 300 in The Indian Penal Code, 1860](https://indiankanoon.org/doc/626019/) ⚠️ snippet only [Indian Kanoon:11] [Vijai Pal And Another vs State Of U.P. on 1 April, 2026](https://indiankanoon.org/doc/83256722/) ⚠️ snippet only [Indian Kanoon:12] [Shoyeb Raja vs State Of Madhya Pradesh on 25 September, 2024](https://indiankanoon.org/doc/74649962/) ⚠️ snippet only

sources.title: Manoj Singh vs State Of M.P. on 10 November, 2021 - Indian Kanoon
sources.content: The intention or knowledge of the accused must be such as is necessary to constitute murder. Without this ingredient being established, there can be no offence of 'attempt to murder'. Under Section 307, the intention precedes the act attributed to accused.

sources.title: Bodha @ Ramesh Turi & Ors vs The State Of West Bengal & Anr ...
sources.content: Where intention or knowledge is the `motivating force' of the act complained of, Section 304A will have to make room for the graver and more serious charge of culpable homicide not amounting to murder or amounting to murder as the facts disclose.

sources.title: Section 300 in The Indian Penal Code, 1860
sources.content: This is murder, in as much as the provocation was not given by the child, and the death of the child was not caused by accident or misfortune in doing an act caused by the provocation.

sources.title: Vijai Pal And Another vs State Of U.P. on 1 April, 2026
sources.content: Apr 1, 2026 · The reliance be placed on the judgment of the case Natthu v. State of U.P., 2019 SCC OnLine All 3547 As observed earlier, in the instant case also it appears that prosecution has concealed true genesis of the incident and failed to establish the circumstances in which the appellants have alleged to have caused injuries to the deceased.

[Search: site:indiankanoon.org "murder" "essential ingredients"]
Search results: 



--- SOURCE 1: Manoj Singh vs State Of M.P. on 10 November, 2021 - Indian Kanoon ---
URL: https://indiankanoon.org/docfragment/128584680/?formInput=murder+ingredients
AUTHORITY_TIER: primary

SUMMARY:
The intention or knowledge of the accused must be such as is necessary to constitute murder. Without this ingredient being established, there can be no offence of 'attempt to murder'. Under Section 307, the intention precedes the act attributed to accused.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 2: Bodha @ Ramesh Turi & Ors vs The State Of West Bengal & Anr ... ---
URL: https://indiankanoon.org/doc/167990544/
AUTHORITY_TIER: primary

SUMMARY:
Where intention or knowledge is the `motivating force' of the act complained of, Section 304A will have to make room for the graver and more serious charge of culpable homicide not amounting to murder or amounting to murder as the facts disclose.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 3: Section 300 in The Indian Penal Code, 1860 ---
URL: https://indiankanoon.org/doc/626019/
AUTHORITY_TIER: primary

SUMMARY:
This is murder, in as much as the provocation was not given by the child, and the death of the child was not caused by accident or misfortune in doing an act caused by the provocation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
